(916) 322·5662 
Charles R. Martin 
Attorney at Law 
P.O. Bo x 885 6 
San Marino, CA 
91108 
Administration 
322·5660 
Executive/legal 
322·5901 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
December 13, 1983 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Re: 
Advice Letter No. A-83-260 
Dear Mr. Martin: 
Thank you for your request for advice on the conflict of 
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act. 
Your questions 
concern city attorneys who are paid, on a retainer basis, to 
attend city council meetings. 
You asked· whether the Act permits 
these city attorneys to also: 
1. 
Recommend to the city council that the city bring or 
defend a lawsuit when it is possible, or even probable, that 
the city council will hire the attorney to perform the work, 
or 
2. 
Process assessment district proceedings, perform 
lobbying activities, issue bond opinions, or perform other 
special work, for which the attorney is paid an additional 
fee. 
DISCUSSION 
Government Code Section 871001/ provides that no local 
public official shall make or participate in the making of a 
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know 
that he has a financial interest. 
An official has a "financial 
interest" in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if 
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect on: 
!I Hereinafter all statutory references are to the 
Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 


Charles R. Martin 
Decemoer 13, 1983 
Page 2 
* * * 
(c) 
Any source of income, ••• aggregating two 
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided 
to, received by or promised to the public official 
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is 
made •••• 
(Section 87103(c).) 
A city attorney is a "public official" as described in 
Section 87100. 2/ 
A decision will have a material financial 
effect on an official's source of income if it will "directly 
affect the amount of income to be received by the official •••• " 
(2 Ca 1. Adm. Code Section 18702 (a) (3) (A) • ) 
However, th e 
definition of "income" specifically excludes "salary and 
reimbursement for expenses or per diem received from a • 
local • • • government agency." 
(Section 82030 (b) (2) • ) 
A city attorney may make recommendations to a city .council 
concerning existing or future legal actions as long as the fee 
to be paid to the attorney for performing the work constitutes 
"salary." 
In the Ritchie advice letter (A-79-045, copy 
attached), it was stated that "payments to a retained city 
attorney on an hourly basis, i.e., tied to the amount of work 
performed for the city, are clearly analogous to the salary 
received by public employees •••• " 
In the case of bond issues, 
attorneys are often paid a fixed percentage of a maximum bond 
issue amount. 
Because the amount to be paid is fixed, and since 
this is the customary payment arrangement, it is sufficiently 
similar in nature to hourly payments and can be considered to be 
salary. 
This result recognizes the nature of the attorney-
client relationship and the need to allow a city attorney to 
advise his clients on future legal actions. 
2/ 
The term "public official" means every member, officer 
employee or consultant of a local government agency. 
(Section 
82048.) 
"Consultant" includes "any natural person who provides, 
under contract, information, advice, recommendation or counsel 
to a • • • local government agency •••• " 
(2 Cal. Adm. Code 
Section 18700 (a) (2) • ) 


Charles R. Martin 
December 13, 1983 
Page 3 
Our advice is, of course, confined to the provisions of the 
Political Reform Act. 
We are not giving advice on any other 
provisions, including Section 1090. 
As you know, the Attorney 
General has spoken on some of these issues in A.G. Opinion, No. 
83-404, October 19, 1983. 
If you have any questions on these other provisions, you 
should consult your County Counsel. 
If you have any additional 
questions on the Political Reform Act, please feel free to 
contact me at (916) 322-5901. 
JSM:plh 
Enclosure 
Very truly yours, 
~~a~:Je(~ 
Counsel 
Legal Division 


OHARI ... ES R. MARTIN 
ATTORNEY AT LAW 
P. O. BOX 8856 
SAN MARINO, CALIFORNIA 91108 
November 14, 1983 
Ms. Barbara Millman 
P.O. Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 95804 
Dear Ms. Millman: 
CITY OF SAN MARINO 282·1155 
CITY OF SOUTH PASADENA 799·9101 
CITY OF IRWINDALE 962·3381 
PERSONAL SECRETARY 285·2685 
Thank you for your recent letter enclosing the Attorney 
General's opinion re Gov. Code, Sec. 1090, with regard to a 
proposed contract. 
I am happy to accept the opinion as far 
as it goes, but it does not answer the questions I sought. 
Mayan attorney who among his clients lists a city or several 
cities for which he serves as City Attorney on a retainer 
basis to attend Council meetings only: 
f 
a. 
Recommend to the City Council the bringing or defending 
of lawsuits when it is possible, even probably, that 
the City Council will wish for that attorney to represent 
the City and be paid therefor? 
b. 
May the City Council employ the City Attorney to process 
assessment district proceedings, lobbying efforts, bond 
opinions, or other special work, andpay that attorney 
additional fees therefor? 
My memo to you indicated that since municipal income is 
exempt under FPPC, it could not act as a possible conflict 
of interest, and cited Miller v. Sac to 66 Cal App 3d 863 
and FPPC #A-83-l52---, but I would appreciate your analysis.

s on the Political Reform Act, please feel free to

JSM:plh 
Enclosure
sections.conclusion: A city attorney may make recommendations to a city council concerning existing or future legal actions as long as the fee to be paid to the attorney for performing the work constitutes 'salary.' Payments to a retained city attorney on an hourly basis are analogous to salary and thus exclude income from the conflict of interest analysis. Bond issue fees fixed as a percentage of the bond amount are similarly excludable as salary.
sections.analysis:
Government Code Section 871001/ provides that no local 
public official shall make or participate in the making of a 
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know 
that he has a financial interest. 
An official has a "financial 
interest" in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if 
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect on: 
!I Hereinafter Charles R. Martin 
Decemoer 13, 1983
sections.question_synthetic: Whether city attorneys paid on a retainer basis to attend city council meetings may recommend legal actions or perform additional work for the city for additional fees without violating conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: City attorneys paid on a retainer or hourly basis may recommend legal actions and perform additional work for fees because such payments constitute 'salary' under the Political Reform Act and are excluded from the financial interest definition, allowing attorneys to advise clients on future legal actions.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: ocr, old; LLM: Document contains both the original incoming request letter (November 14, 1983) and the FPPC response letter (December 13, 1983). OCR quality issues in some portions but main content clearly extracted. Reference to Attorney General Opinion 83-404 and FPPC Advice Letter A-79-045 (Ritchie) provided.

Enclosure

CONCLUSION: A city attorney may make recommendations to a city council concerning existing or future legal actions as long as the fee to be paid to the attorney for performing the work constitutes 'salary.' Payments to a retained city attorney on an hourly basis are analogous to salary and thus exclude income from the conflict of interest analysis. Bond issue fees fixed as a percentage of the bond amount are similarly excludable as salary.

embedding.summary: FPPC Advice Letter A-83-260 addresses whether city attorneys on retainer can recommend legal actions and perform additional fee-based work without conflicts of interest. The FPPC concludes that hourly retainer payments and fixed-percentage bond fees constitute 'salary' and are excluded from the conflict of interest provisions, permitting such activities.
